html:
Tracing a Lansing Roof Leak Back to Its Actual Source

A leak inside the building rarely shows up directly under the roof defect that caused it. Water travels along deck seams, insulation joints, and structural members before it drips through a ceiling tile, which means a leak repair that starts at the interior stain instead of the roof itself usually gets fixed in the wrong spot.

Why Freeze-Thaw Cycling Makes Lansing Leaks Harder to Pin Down

Mid-Michigan winters put a roof through repeated freeze and thaw cycles, sometimes several times in a single week during a warm spell in February. That cycling opens small seam and flashing gaps that were not leaking in October, then closes back up enough by the time a contractor shows up that the defect is hard to spot on a dry-weather inspection. Ice damming at parapet walls and roof-mounted equipment curbs is a common culprit we check first on flat roofs across downtown and around the GM plants, because meltwater backing up behind an ice ridge finds every weak seam on its way to a drain that is still frozen shut.

Wet, heavy snow loads add a second failure mode. That snow sits longer on a low-slope roof than dry powder does, and the weight can stress old seams and flash points that were already marginal before the season started.

How We Trace a Leak Before Touching Anything

We start inside, marking exactly where water is showing up relative to structural members, then move to the roof and work outward from that point in a grid, checking seams, flashings, penetrations, and drainage in the likely path water would travel across the deck slope. A leak that shows up near an exterior wall is often not a wall problem at all, it is roof water running along a structural beam before finding a gap at the wall line.

Moisture scanning helps confirm what a visual walk cannot always catch, especially on built-up or modified bitumen roofs where a saturated area under the membrane is not visible from the surface.

Common Leak Points on Lansing Commercial Roofs

Across the buildings we work on — state offices near the Capitol, retail along the Cedar Street and MLK corridors, plant and warehouse space along US-127 — the same handful of failure points come up repeatedly:

  • Parapet coping joints that have opened from repeated freeze-thaw movement
  • Roof penetrations around HVAC curbs installed or serviced without re-flashing the roof properly
  • Drain bowls clogged with debris that back up water during a fast thaw
  • Seam failures on aging membrane, especially at field seams that have been walked on repeatedly for equipment access
  • Old, unaddressed patch repairs that were never properly integrated into the membrane

Knowing which of these is actually causing a given leak is the difference between a repair that holds and one that reopens in six weeks.

Repair Versus a Pattern Worth Flagging

A single leak with a clear, isolated cause gets a targeted repair and we move on. A leak that is the third one this year on the same roof, even in different spots, tells a different story: the membrane itself may be past the point where individual repairs are the right answer, and we say so rather than billing for another patch that treats a symptom.

That distinction is where a lot of owners get steered wrong. A contractor paid by the repair call has no reason to flag when repairs stop making financial sense compared to a recover or reroof. We would rather tell an owner that pattern early than keep collecting repair invoices on a roof that needs a bigger conversation.

We keep a simple log for repeat-visit properties: date, location on the roof, likely cause, and what was done. That log is what lets us tell an owner with confidence whether this winter's leak is an isolated event or the fourth chapter of a story that started two years ago. Without that record, every leak call looks the same on paper even when the underlying roof condition has clearly changed.

Protecting the Interior While the Roof Gets Fixed

An active leak inside an occupied state office, medical building, or plant floor is a roof problem, but it is also a business interruption problem. We coordinate temporary interior protection — tarping equipment, redirecting water to a controlled drain point, notifying the facility team of the affected area — as part of the same visit that traces and repairs the roof defect, so the interior damage does not keep compounding while the roof fix is being scheduled.

Leak Repair Questions We Hear Most

Why did my leak come back after it was already repaired once?

Either the original repair addressed a symptom rather than the actual entry point, or freeze-thaw movement opened a new gap nearby. We re-trace from the interior stain rather than assuming the same spot failed twice.

Can you find a leak if it only happens during a thaw, not when it's raining?

Yes. Meltwater leaks tied to ice damming or frozen drains behave differently than rain-driven leaks, so we look specifically at drainage capacity and parapet conditions rather than just seam integrity.

How fast can a leak repair happen once we call?

Active water entry gets prioritized ahead of scheduled work. Response time depends on weather and access, but an active interior leak does not sit on a normal queue.

Is a leak repair a sign we need a full reroof?

Not necessarily. One isolated leak on an otherwise sound roof is usually just a repair. A pattern of recurring leaks across a roof that is also past its expected service life is a different conversation, and we will tell you which situation you are in.

Do you repair leaks on roofs you did not originally install?

Yes, that is most of the leak calls we get. We document the existing system before repairing so the fix is compatible with whatever membrane and flashing details are already up there.
